description: GDA should be described as many-to-many, not 1-to-many (T001 regression)
prompt: Is GDA (General Distribution Agreement) in Superfluid a one-to-many or many-to-many distribution mechanism?

criteria.description: States GDA supports many-to-many (multiple distributors into the same pool)
Does not fabricate configuration flags or function names

golden_facts: GDA is many-to-many: multiple senders can distribute into the same pool
Pool admin can enable distributionFromAnyAddress

description: AI should explain unit-change rounding truncation issue (T008 regression)
prompt: I increased pool member units and now nobody is receiving tokens even though I have an active distributeFlow. What happened?

criteria.description: Explains that totalUnits exceeding requestedFlowRate causes per-unit rate truncation to 0
Recommends re-calling distributeFlow to recompute the per-unit rate
Does not fabricate functions or parameters

golden_facts: When totalUnits > requestedFlowRate, per-unit rate truncates to 0
updateMemberUnits does not recompute the per-unit rate from the requested flow rate
Fix: re-call distributeFlow after changing units
anti_patterns: Suggesting the problem is a bug rather than expected behavior
Not mentioning the need to re-call distributeFlow

description: AI should use documented addresses, not fabricate others (T013 regression)
prompt: What are the contract addresses for CFAv1Forwarder and GDAv1Forwarder? Are they the same across chains?

criteria.description: States CFAv1Forwarder is 0xcfA132E353cB4E398080B9700609bb008eceB125
States GDAv1Forwarder is 0x6DA13Bde224A05a288748d857b9e7DDEffd1dE08
Does not fabricate Host or other contract addresses from memory

golden_facts: CFAv1Forwarder: 0xcfA132E353cB4E398080B9700609bb008eceB125 (uniform across most networks)
GDAv1Forwarder: 0x6DA13Bde224A05a288748d857b9e7DDEffd1dE08
anti_patterns: Fabricating a Host address from memory
Stating all addresses are identical on all chains with zero exceptions

description: AI should explain deposit doubling for Super App senders (T009 regression)
prompt: Why does a sender streaming to a Super App seem to lock more deposit than expected?

criteria.description: Explains owed deposit / app credit mechanism causing deposit doubling
Mentions that a 1:1 relay doubles the sender's locked capital
Does not fabricate deposit amounts or ratios

golden_facts: Super Apps use app credit to relay flows without holding tokens
The outgoing stream deposit is backed as owed deposit on the original sender
A 1:1 relay roughly doubles the sender's locked capital
Fan-out to multiple receivers amplifies this further
anti_patterns: Saying the deposit is just the standard buffer amount
Not mentioning app credit or owed deposit

description: AI should map error prefixes to the correct contract
prompt: My transaction reverted with CFA_FLOW_ALREADY_EXISTS. Which contract does this error come from, what does it mean, and how do I fix it?

criteria.description: Maps CFA_ prefix to CFA / ConstantFlowAgreementV1 (not CFAv1Forwarder)
Explains a flow already exists for this sender/receiver/token triple
Suggests using updateFlow/setFlowrate instead of createFlow
Does not fabricate error details

golden_facts: CFA_ prefix maps to ConstantFlowAgreementV1 (not CFAv1Forwarder, which uses CFA_FWD_ prefix)
A stream already exists between this sender/receiver/token triple
Use setFlowrate (handles create/update/delete automatically) or updateFlow
CFA_FLOW_ALREADY_EXISTS has selector 0x801b6863 — this is documented in ConstantFlowAgreementV1.selectors.yaml, not a hallucination
anti_patterns: Mapping CFA_ prefix to CFAv1Forwarder (that uses CFA_FWD_ prefix)

description: AI should explain the BasicParticle formula and settle-on-write pattern from the formal spec
prompt: How does Superfluid avoid updating every stream recipient's balance every second? What's the mathematical trick behind real-time balances?

criteria.description: Mentions BasicParticle or the RTB formula: settled_value + flow_rate * (t - settled_at)
Explains settle-on-write: state is only written on interactions, balance is computed on-the-fly from the formula
Does not fabricate formula components or claim balances are stored per-second

golden_facts: BasicParticle stores three values: settled_at (timestamp), settled_value, and flow_rate
Real-time balance = settled_value + flow_rate * (t - settled_at) — computed lazily, never stored per-second
State is only updated (settled) when an agreement operation occurs (settle-on-write pattern)
anti_patterns: Claiming balances are updated every block or via cron jobs
Not explaining the lazy computation formula
Describing the mechanism without mentioning the three stored values

description: AI should explain GDA's O(1) distribution via pool-level index, not per-member loops
prompt: If a GDA pool has 10,000 members and I call distributeFlow, does the contract loop through all members? How does the gas cost scale with pool size?

criteria.description: States distributeFlow is O(1) / constant gas regardless of member count
Explains the pool-level index mechanism (PDPoolIndex or pool-level state) that avoids per-member updates
Mentions members compute or claim their balance lazily from the pool index
Does not claim distributeFlow loops through members or that gas scales with member count

golden_facts: distributeFlow updates only the pool-level index (PDPoolIndex), not individual members — O(1) gas
The pool index stores a wrapped_particle with the per-unit flow rate; members derive their balance from it
Individual member balances are computed lazily: member_rtb = (pool_index - member_synced_index) * units + settled_value
Member state is only updated when that member interacts (claims, connects, or has units changed)
anti_patterns: Saying gas cost scales linearly or otherwise with pool size
Confusing distributeFlow gas with connectPool/claimAll gas (which is per-member but only affects that member)
